common_name: Crimson Queen Japanese Maple (Red Weeping Japanese Maple)

planting.note: Plant container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, humus-rich soil in dappled to partial shade; in zone 9, east-facing exposures with morning sun and afternoon shade are essential — Crimson Queen is more sensitive to summer heat than upright red Japanese maples.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, humus-rich soil in dappled to partial shade; Crimson Queen is the most popular and widely planted laceleaf (dissectum) weeping Japanese maple with red foliage — a mounding, cascading, mushroom-shaped small tree reaching 6–10 feet tall with an even wider spread, producing deeply dissected, lace-like, rich crimson-red to burgundy-red leaves that maintain better summer color than most dissectum Japanese maples. The extremely finely cut, feathery foliage drapes from gracefully weeping branches in a soft, cascading curtain of crimson-red — one of the most visually dramatic and elegant small trees available for woodland garden or specimen use. The weeping, mounding habit creates a naturally sculptural form without pruning, and mature specimens become increasingly dramatic focal points over decades. In fall, the foliage transitions to bright scarlet. In zone 9, Crimson Queen requires careful siting to succeed — morning sun and afternoon shade in a location with moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil are the minimum requirements. Plant near water features, in east-facing beds, or under the high canopy of larger trees. Provide consistent moisture during summer. In the right location, Crimson Queen is reliably successful in zone 9 and is widely grown in Houston, New Orleans, and coastal Mississippi.

common_name: Pink Perfection Camellia (Shell Pink Camellia japonica)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal in zone 9 — deep shade reduces bloom production while direct afternoon sun causes leaf scorch and flower bud drop.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Pink Perfection is a classic, long-cultivated Camellia japonica cultivar — one of the most widely grown and beloved camellias in American Southern horticulture — producing perfectly formed, small to medium, fully double, shell-pink to pale-blush-pink flowers with tightly spiraling petals arranged in a formal decorative pattern of exceptional precision and beauty. The shell-pink color is softer and more refined than the deeper rose-pink of many camellias, with a cool, porcelain quality that has made Pink Perfection a perennial favorite for cut-flower use and formal garden settings. Bloom season in zone 9 runs from mid-season through late season (January through March), making Pink Perfection one of the later-blooming reliable japonica cultivars — an attribute that makes it more likely to miss the worst freeze events in zone 9. The growth habit is upright and moderately vigorous to 5–10 feet tall with dense, glossy dark-green evergreen foliage. Pink Perfection is considered one of the most reliable japonica camellia cultivars for zone 9 performance, with consistent flower production and good disease resistance. Fertilize with azalea-camellia specific acidic fertilizer in spring and fall; maintain 3–4 inches of pine bark mulch over the shallow root zone year-round.

common_name: Alba Plena Camellia (White Formal Double Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Alba Plena is one of the earliest-blooming japonica camellias — November through January in zone 9 — so site away from frost pockets where early-season cold snaps could damage open flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Alba Plena is one of the oldest and most historically significant Camellia japonica cultivars in cultivation — an early-season white camellia with perfectly formed, large, formal double flowers in pure white with tightly spiraled, overlapping petals arranged in a geometrically precise rosette pattern of extraordinary elegance. The pure white flowers are exceptional for formal garden compositions, white-themed borders, and cut-flower use — few winter-blooming plants can rival the pristine beauty of fully open Alba Plena flowers. In zone 9, it blooms from November through January, making it one of the earliest reliable winter blooms in the Southern garden. The vigorous, upright growth to 5–12 feet produces dense, glossy dark-green foliage that provides excellent year-round screening and foundation value. Alba Plena was introduced to Western cultivation from China in 1792 and has been grown in Southern American gardens since the early nineteenth century — one of the most historically long-cultivated garden plants in the South. Early bloom season means open flowers may occasionally be damaged by hard frosts in zone 9 winters; siting under the canopy of overhead trees provides some protection. Maintain acidic soil pH with periodic applications of acidic fertilizer and deep organic mulch.

common_name: Catawba Crape Myrtle (Deep Purple Crape Myrtle)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Catawba is a medium-sized National Arboretum crape myrtle reaching 10–15 feet — allow adequate space at planting and never top-prune.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Catawba is a National Arboretum hybrid crape myrtle producing one of the deepest, richest purple flower colors available in crape myrtles — a vivid, true violet-purple that holds color through the heat of the Gulf South summer without fading to pink or lavender. Purple is among the rarest and most sought-after crape myrtle flower colors — most purple-claiming cultivars prove to be lavender, mauve, or reddish-purple — but Catawba's color is among the most reliably true purple in cultivation. The flowers are produced in large, compact clusters from June through September in zone 9, covering a medium-sized multi-trunked tree to 10–15 feet with vivid purple bloom. Fall foliage transitions to attractive orange-red. The exfoliating bark develops an attractive mottled pattern of gray and tan with age. Like other National Arboretum hybrid crape myrtles, Catawba shows good resistance to powdery mildew. Its medium size makes it appropriate for the majority of residential landscapes where the large tree-form Natchez and Muskogee would eventually overwhelm the space. Catawba is an excellent choice for the gardener who wants a distinctive crape myrtle that stands apart from the dominant pink and red cultivars. Never top or severely prune — proper pruning removes only dead and crossing branches.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program

common_name: Hopi Crape Myrtle (Medium Pink Semi-Dwarf Crape Myrtle)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Hopi is a compact National Arboretum selection reaching 7–10 feet — an excellent size for residential foundations and mixed shrub plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Hopi is a compact, semi-dwarf National Arboretum crape myrtle that reaches 7–10 feet — smaller than the tree-sized Tonto and Sioux but larger than the truly dwarf compact selections — producing medium-pink to clear rose-pink flowers from July through September in dense, upright clusters. The clear, medium-pink flower color is one of the cleanest and most pleasing pinks in the crape myrtle palette — brighter and more saturated than the light lavender-pink of the large-tree Muskogee but softer and more feminine than the hot-pink Sioux. Hopi has perhaps the best mildew resistance rating of any National Arboretum crape myrtle, making it especially valuable in humid Gulf Coast climates where powdery mildew can be a persistent problem with older crape myrtle cultivars. The compact, upright-spreading habit with attractive exfoliating bark and reliable orange to red fall foliage provides year-round interest. Hopi's manageable size makes it one of the most versatile crape myrtles for typical residential lot sizes, appropriate for foundation plantings, screening masses, and specimen use without requiring restriction pruning that destroys the natural form. Remove only dead and crossing branches; never top.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program
